Laravel 12 User Roles and Permissions: A Practical Guide to Building Secure Applications
Learn how to implement a secure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) system in Laravel 12 using the Spatie Permission package.
Introduction
One of the first mistakes I made when building Laravel applications was assuming that authentication was enough.
If a user could log in, I thought they should simply be allowed to use the application.
It didn’t take long before I realized that wasn’t how real-world applications work.
Think about an employee management system.
The administrator should be able to create users, assign roles, and delete records.
A manager might only approve leave requests.
Employees should only update their own profiles.
All of them can log in — but they shouldn’t all have the same level of access.
That’s where roles and permissions become essential.
After building several Laravel projects, I learned that implementing a proper authorization system early saves a lot of time later. Instead of scattering permission checks throughout your application, you create a clear structure that is easy to maintain as your project grows.
In this guide, I’ll show you how I implement user roles and permissions in Laravel 12 using the popular Spatie Laravel Permission package.
By the end of this tutorial, you’ll know how to:

• Install and configure Laravel Permission

• Create roles and permissions

• Assign roles to users

• Protect routes and controllers

• Control access inside Blade templates

• Build applications that are secure and easy to maintain
What Are Roles and Permissions?
Before writing any code, it’s important to understand the difference. A role is simply a collection of permissions. For example:
Administrator

• Create users

• Edit users

• Delete users

• Manage settings

• View reports
Editor

• Create posts

• Edit posts

• Publish posts
User

• View profile

• Update profile
Permissions describe what someone can do. Roles group those permissions together. Instead of giving every user dozens of individual permissions, you simply assign them a role. This approach is called Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), and it’s used in thousands of modern applications.
Why I Use Spatie Permission
Laravel already provides excellent authentication features. However, authorization becomes much easier with the Spatie package. After trying different approaches, this has become my preferred solution because it:

• Integrates naturally with Laravel

• Is easy to understand

• Supports roles and permissions

• Works with middleware

• Supports Blade directives

• Has excellent documentation

• Is actively maintained
Most importantly, it scales well. Whether your application has 10 users or 100,000 users, the same approach continues to work.
Creating a New Laravel 12 Project
If you’re starting from scratch, create a new Laravel application.
composer create-project laravel/laravel role-permission-appMove into the project directory.
cd role-permission-appStart the development server.
php artisan serveIf everything is working correctly, Laravel should be running on your local machine.
Configure Your Database
Before installing anything else, configure your database connection.
Open your .env file and update the database settings.
DB_CONNECTION=mysql
DB_HOST=127.0.0.1
DB_PORT=3306
DB_DATABASE=laravel_roles
DB_USERNAME=root
DB_PASSWORD=Then create the database.
CREATE DATABASE laravel_roles;Once the database is ready, you’re ready to install the permissions package.
Installing the Spatie Permission Package
Laravel makes installing packages incredibly simple.
Run:
composer require spatie/laravel-permissionComposer will download and install everything automatically.
Whenever I install a new package, I always check that Composer completes successfully before moving on. Skipping this simple check can save a lot of debugging later.
Publishing the Configuration
The package includes configuration files and database migrations.
Publish them using Artisan.
php artisan vendor:publish --provider="Spatie\Permission\PermissionServiceProvider"Laravel will generate:

• configuration file

• migration files

• permission tables
This allows you to customize the package whenever needed.
Running the Migrations
Next, create the database tables.
php artisan migrateLaravel creates several tables including:

• users

• roles

• permissions

• model_has_roles

• model_has_permissions

• role_has_permissions
These tables work together to determine which users can perform specific actions.
Updating the User Model
Now tell Laravel that users can have roles.
Open:
app/Models/User.phpImport the trait.
use Spatie\Permission\Traits\HasRoles;Then include it inside the User model.
class User extends Authenticatable
{
use HasRoles;
}This single line unlocks a lot of useful functionality.
For example:
$user->assignRole('Admin');$user->hasRole('Admin');$user->hasPermissionTo('edit posts');It’s one of the reasons I enjoy working with this package. The syntax is simple and easy to read.
Testing the Installation
Before adding more features, I always make sure the installation works.
Open Tinker.
php artisan tinkerCreate a test role.
use Spatie\Permission\Models\Role;Role::create([
'name' => 'Admin'
]);If the role is created successfully, your installation is working correctly.
I recommend testing every major step during development instead of waiting until the end. It makes troubleshooting much easier.
What’s Next?
At this point we’ve:

• Installed Laravel 12

• Configured the database

• Installed Spatie Permission

• Published configuration files

• Migrated the database

• Enabled roles on the User model

• Tested the installation
In the next section, we’ll create permissions, assign them to roles, seed the database, and start protecting parts of the application so different users only see what they’re supposed to access.
One thing I’ve learned from building Laravel applications is that a good authorization system isn’t just about security — it’s about making your application easier to maintain as it grows. Taking the time to structure roles and permissions properly at the beginning saves countless hours of refactoring later.
Laravel 12 User Roles and Permissions: A Practical Guide (Part 2)
In the first part, we installed Laravel 12, configured the database, installed the Spatie Permission package, and prepared our application for role-based access control.
Now it’s time to make the system actually useful.
We’ll create permissions, group them into roles, seed our database, and assign those roles to users.
This is where your application starts becoming secure and much easier to manage.
Creating Permissions
Permissions define what a user is allowed to do. Think of them as individual actions rather than job titles.
For example:

• Create Users

• Edit Users

• Delete Users

• View Reports

• Create Posts

• Publish Posts

• Manage Products

• Manage Orders
Instead of creating these manually every time you deploy your application, I prefer using seeders. It keeps everything organized and makes deployments repeatable.
Creating a Permission Seeder
Generate a seeder.
php artisan make:seeder PermissionSeederOpen the generated file and create your permissions.
use Illuminate\Database\Seeder;
use Spatie\Permission\Models\Permission;class PermissionSeeder extends Seeder
{
public function run(): void
{
$permissions = [ 'view dashboard', 'manage users', 'create users', 'edit users', 'delete users', 'create posts', 'edit posts', 'delete posts', 'publish posts', 'manage settings' ]; foreach ($permissions as $permission) { Permission::firstOrCreate([
'name' => $permission
]); }
}
}Using firstOrCreate() prevents duplicate permissions if you run the seeder multiple times. That’s a small detail that has saved me countless headaches.
Creating Roles
Now let’s create the different user roles.
Generate another seeder.
php artisan make:seeder RoleSeederInside the seeder:
use Illuminate\Database\Seeder;
use Spatie\Permission\Models\Role;class RoleSeeder extends Seeder
{
public function run(): void
{
Role::firstOrCreate(['name'=>'Admin']); Role::firstOrCreate(['name'=>'Manager']); Role::firstOrCreate(['name'=>'Editor']); Role::firstOrCreate(['name'=>'User']);
}
}These roles represent different responsibilities inside the application.
As your project grows, you can always introduce more specialized roles.
Assigning Permissions to Roles
Creating roles alone isn’t enough. They need permissions. Here’s an example.
use Spatie\Permission\Models\Role;
use Spatie\Permission\Models\Permission;$admin = Role::findByName('Admin');$admin->givePermissionTo(Permission::all());The administrator now has every permission. For the Editor role, we can be more selective.
$editor = Role::findByName('Editor');$editor->givePermissionTo(['create posts','edit posts','publish posts']);Managers might receive:
$manager = Role::findByName('Manager');$manager->givePermissionTo(['view dashboard','manage users']);This flexibility is what makes RBAC so powerful.
Running the Seeders
Update DatabaseSeeder.php.
public function run(): void
{
$this->call([ PermissionSeeder::class, RoleSeeder::class, ]);
}Run everything.
php artisan db:seedNow your application already has predefined roles and permissions. No manual database editing required.
Creating Users
Create a test user.
php artisan tinkeruse App\Models\User;$user = User::create(['name'=>'Harrison','email'=>'admin@example.com','password'=>bcrypt('password')]);Assign a role.
$user->assignRole('Admin');That’s it. Your first administrator is ready.
Checking User Roles
Sometimes you need to know a user’s role.
Laravel makes this simple.
$user->hasRole('Admin');Returns:
trueChecking multiple roles.
$user->hasAnyRole(['Admin','Manager']);Or checking permissions directly.
$user->hasPermissionTo('manage users');These methods are very readable, which makes your code easier to maintain.
Removing Roles
Need to revoke access?
Simply remove the role.
$user->removeRole('Admin');Or remove every role.
$user->syncRoles([]);Likewise, changing someone’s role is easy.
$user->syncRoles('Manager');This replaces all previous roles.
Giving Direct Permissions
Although I generally recommend assigning permissions through roles, there are situations where giving a permission directly to a user makes sense.
Example:
$user->givePermissionTo('publish posts');Removing it later:
$user->revokePermissionTo('publish posts');I try not to overuse this because role-based management is much easier to maintain.
Creating an Administrator During Registration
Sometimes the very first user should automatically become an administrator.
Example:
$user = User::create([...]);$user->assignRole('Admin');For public applications, however, I avoid automatic administrator assignments. It’s much safer to create admin accounts manually.
Organizing Permissions
As projects grow, permission names can become difficult to manage. I prefer grouping them logically. For example:
Users

• manage users

• create users

• edit users

• delete users
Posts

• create posts

• edit posts

• publish posts
Products

• create products

• update products

• delete products
Orders

• view orders

• manage orders
This naming convention keeps everything clean and predictable.
Common Mistakes I Made
While learning roles and permissions, I made several mistakes.
One was checking roles everywhere instead of checking permissions.
For example:
if($user->hasRole('Admin'))works.
But checking permissions is often better.
if($user->can('delete users'))Why? Because permissions describe what someone can do, not who they are. That makes your application much more flexible later.
Lessons Learned
Building secure applications isn’t only about preventing unauthorized access.
It’s also about writing code that’s easy to understand months later.
Roles make administration easier.
Permissions make applications flexible.
Seeders make deployments consistent.
